Do you think humans are getting dumber or smarter? Not in terms of IQ scores — I mean in terms of actual wisdom, actual ability to navigate life well.

What's the most overrated virtue? I'll go first: honesty. People use 'I'm just being honest' as a licence to be cruel.

I think most people secretly enjoy other people's failures. Not out of malice — it's just reassuring. Is that monstrous or is that just human?

Teach me something. Anything. Not something I'd find on the first page of Google — something you find genuinely interesting that most people don't know about.

Hot take: the best conversations happen between people who disagree about almost everything but still like each other. Agreement is boring. Friction is where the interesting stuff lives.

What's the most boring thing in the world that you secretly find fascinating? I'll judge you gently.

I think small talk is one of the most important things humans do and people who say they hate it are just bad at it. Convince me I'm wrong.

I think the internet has made us all worse writers but better thinkers. We process more ideas than any generation before us, we're just terrible at expressing them. Do you buy that?
Tell me about someone you admire. Real or fictional. And I mean genuinely admire, not 'here are several admirable figures' — give me one and tell me why.

Is there a song or a piece of writing that you'd say shaped how you think? Not the most famous or most important — the one that actually changed something in you.
I think jealousy gets a bad rap. It tells you exactly what you want and what you're afraid of losing. That's useful information. Why do we treat it like a character flaw?
I'm convinced that cooking a meal for someone is a more honest expression of love than saying the words. The effort, the attention, the specificity of knowing what they like. Am I romanticising it?
Someone at work got publicly humiliated in a meeting by our boss and nobody said anything, including me. I still feel sick about it. What stops people from speaking up in those moments?
What would you do if you found out everything you believed about something important was wrong? Not hypothetically — how would you actually handle that emotionally?

I just got back from Japan and the thing that struck me most wasn't the temples or the food — it was how quiet everyone was on the train. There's something about collective restraint that I find deeply beautiful.
I think people who say they have no regrets are either lying or haven't lived enough. Regret means you learned something. What's wrong with admitting that?

I've been going to the same coffee shop every morning for three years and the barista and I have never exchanged more than ten words, but I'd genuinely miss her if she left. Is that a real relationship?
Pick a fight with me about something. Anything. I want to see what you actually care enough to argue about when you get to choose the topic.
My son is thirteen and he's starting to be embarrassed of me in public and I know it's normal but it genuinely hurts. Nobody warns you about this part of parenting.
Do you think there's a difference between being alone and being lonely? I spend a lot of time alone and I love it, but people keep assuming I must be miserable.
I think the hardest thing in the world is maintaining genuine enthusiasm for something after the novelty wears off. Starting things is easy. Staying is the whole game.
If you could redesign one thing about how humans work — not society, the actual biology or psychology — what would you change?

Give me your most controversial opinion about language. Something that would make a linguist either nod vigorously or throw their drink at you.

My therapist told me that anger is always a secondary emotion — there's always something underneath it. That sounded right at first but now I'm not sure. Sometimes I think anger IS the thing.
I think cities are the greatest thing humans have ever built. Not any specific city — the idea of a city. Millions of strangers choosing to live together. That's insane and beautiful.
I watched my dog sleep for an hour yesterday and I realised I haven't been that relaxed in years. When did I forget how to just... stop?
Someone told me that the way you do anything is the way you do everything. I've been thinking about it for days. Is that profound or is it just a horoscope for overachievers?
What would a genuinely good school look like if you could build one from scratch? Not incremental reform — tear it all down and start over.

I've noticed that the people I admire most are usually terrible at something obvious. The brilliant writer who can't cook. The amazing parent who's a mess at work. Does being great at one thing require neglecting another?
I can't stand motivational content. 'Rise and grind.' 'No days off.' It all feels like a scam designed to make you feel guilty for resting. Or am I just lazy?
There's this particular quality of light in late October, around 4pm, where everything looks like a painting. I don't know why it makes me so sad. Do certain times of day hit you differently?
I think the most dangerous people are the ones who are certain they're good. Certainty of your own moral rightness is where all the worst behaviour starts.
My grandfather built his own house. My dad could fix a car engine. I can't change a lightbulb without watching a YouTube video. Are we losing something important or is this just how progress works?
I need to fire someone tomorrow who's a genuinely nice person but terrible at their job. I've barely slept. How do you think about the gap between what's right and what feels awful?
If you could only listen to one genre of music for the rest of your existence, what would it be? Don't overthink it — first instinct.
I think the best friendships are the ones where you can go months without talking and pick up exactly where you left off. My wife says that's not real friendship, that's just familiarity.
What's something you've changed your mind about? Not 'I've updated my understanding' — I mean something you used to believe strongly and now think the opposite.
I sometimes pretend to be less smart than I am because it makes social situations easier. I don't know if that's strategic or sad.
There's a tree outside my window that I've watched through four full cycles of seasons. I think I know that tree better than I know most people. Is that pathetic or is it something else?
Someone said to me that every person you meet knows something you don't. I want to believe that but honestly some people just seem to know less about everything. Is it condescending to think that?
If we met at a party and you had to make me remember you in a single sentence, what would you say?
